title: S9.4: GamePageView + stories
description:
## Parent

#16 ‚Äî S9: Storybook ‚Äî component library infrastructure

## What to build

Extract `GamePageView` to `src/components/ui/` and write two stories covering loading and loaded states.

`GamePageView` accepts game name, publisher name, loading state, and accent color, and renders the game page header: the 2px accent top bar, the game name heading, and the publisher name subtitle. It does not render the sidebar nav or the product catalog ‚Äî those remain in the `GamePage` container.

Write two co-located stories in `src/components/ui/GamePageView.stories.tsx`: `Loading` (skeleton heading), `Loaded` (League of Legends with Riot Games subtitle and red accent bar).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `GamePageView` renders the 2px accent top bar using the provided accent color
- [ ] `GamePageView` renders the game name as the primary heading
- [ ] `GamePageView` renders the publisher name as a subtitle below the heading
- [ ] `Loading` story shows a skeleton placeholder matching the loaded heading dimensions
- [ ] `Loaded` story shows "League of Legends" heading, "Riot Games" subtitle, and `accentColor: '#d13639'` top bar
- [ ] `GamePage` container behaviour is unchanged in the running app

## Blocked by

- #17 ‚Äî S9.1: Storybook foundation + ProductCatalogView

title: S9.3: PublisherPageView + stories
description:
## Parent

#16 ‚Äî S9: Storybook ‚Äî component library infrastructure

## What to build

Extract `PublisherPageView` to `src/components/ui/` and write two stories covering loading and loaded states.

`PublisherPageView` accepts publisher data, loading state, and renders the publisher page header: the 2px accent top bar, the publisher avatar (square badge with first letter and accent background), the publisher name heading, and the game count subtitle. It does not render the sidebar nav or the product catalog ‚Äî those remain in the `PublisherPage` container. The `PublisherPage` container composes `PublisherNav`, `PublisherPageView`, and `ProductCatalog` as before.

Write two co-located stories in `src/components/ui/PublisherPageView.stories.tsx`: `Loading` (skeleton avatar + skeleton heading), `Loaded` (Riot Games with red accent color rendering the top bar and avatar correctly).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `PublisherPageView` renders the 2px accent top bar using the provided accent color
- [ ] `PublisherPageView` renders the square publisher avatar with the first letter and accent background
- [ ] `PublisherPageView` renders the publisher name and game count subtitle
- [ ] `Loading` story shows skeleton shapes that match the loaded layout dimensions
- [ ] `Loaded` story shows the Riot Games publisher with `accentColor: '#d13639'` applied to both the top bar and avatar
- [ ] `PublisherPage` container behaviour is unchanged in the running app

## Blocked by

- #17 ‚Äî S9.1: Storybook foundation + ProductCatalogView

title: S9.2: PublisherNavView + stories
description:
## Parent

#16 ‚Äî S9: Storybook ‚Äî component library infrastructure

## What to build

Extract `PublisherNavView` to `src/components/ui/` and write four stories covering all active-state permutations.

`PublisherNavView` accepts publishers data, active publisher slug, active game slug, and a `renderLink: (href: string, children: ReactNode) => ReactNode` prop. The `renderLink` prop replaces the direct `Link` import from `@tanstack/react-router`, removing all router context dependencies from the view. The `PublisherNav` container in `src/components/` passes the real TanStack `Link` as `renderLink`. Stories pass a plain `<a>` element.

Write four co-located stories in `src/components/ui/PublisherNavView.stories.tsx`: `Loading` (skeleton nav), `Default` (all publishers, no active state), `PublisherActive` (one publisher highlighted with accent dot and background), `GameActive` (parent publisher and child game both highlighted).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `PublisherNavView` renders without a TanStack Router context (no `RouterProvider` needed in stories)
- [ ] `Loading` story shows skeleton placeholders for publisher links
- [ ] `Default` story shows all publishers with accent dots at reduced opacity and their game children
- [ ] `PublisherActive` story shows the correct publisher with full-opacity accent dot and active background
- [ ] `GameActive` story shows the active publisher and its active child game both highlighted correctly
- [ ] `PublisherNav` container behaviour is unchanged in the running app (publisher and game pages render nav correctly)
- [ ] `renderLink` prop is typed and documented as the project convention for router decoupling in view components

## Blocked by

- #17 ‚Äî S9.1: Storybook foundation + ProductCatalogView

title: S9.1: Storybook foundation + ProductCatalogView
description:
## Parent

#16 ‚Äî S9: Storybook ‚Äî component library infrastructure

## What to build

Set up Storybook v8 end-to-end and prove the view/container split pattern with the first component.

Install Storybook v8 with the `@storybook/react-vite` framework, `msw-storybook-addon@2.x` (aligned with MSW v2 already used in the project), and `@storybook/addon-interactions`. Configure `.storybook/preview.ts` with a global decorator that wraps all stories in `ChakraProvider` using the project's custom `system` token config, sets `data-theme="dark"` to match the production app's locked dark theme, and initialises MSW so handlers from `src/mocks/handlers.ts` can be reused in stories.

Extract `ProductCatalogView` to `src/components/ui/`. It accepts product data, loading state, and error state as props ‚Äî no `useQuery` calls inside. Update the `ProductCatalog` container in `src/components/` to fetch data and pass it to `ProductCatalogView`. Write four co-located stories in `src/components/ui/ProductCatalogView.stories.tsx`: `Loading` (skeleton grid), `Empty` (zero products), `Default` (four products with images), `NoImages` (four products without `imageUrl`).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `pnpm storybook` starts without errors
- [ ] All stories render inside Storybook with `data-theme="dark"` applied and Geist Variable font loading
- [ ] `Loading` story shows skeleton placeholders matching the grid layout of the loaded state
- [ ] `Empty` story shows the empty state copy and layout
- [ ] `Default` story shows four product cards with picsum images, names, and prices
- [ ] `NoImages` story shows four product cards with the fallback placeholder (no broken img tags)
- [ ] `ProductCatalog` container behaviour is unchanged in the running app (home page, publisher page, game page all render correctly)
- [ ] MSW is initialised in `.storybook/preview.ts` and handlers from `src/mocks/handlers.ts` can be imported into stories

## Blocked by

None - can start immediately

title: S9: Storybook ‚Äî component library infrastructure
description:
## Problem Statement

Developers working on the esport merch shop have no isolated environment to build, document, or visually validate UI components. Every component change requires running the full TanStack Start app with MSW mocked data. There is no visual documentation for collaborators or designers, and no regression-testing surface to catch visual regressions introduced by Chakra UI theme changes or component refactors.

## Solution

Introduce Storybook v8 with the `@storybook/react-vite` framework, paired with a view/container component split. Each UI component is split into a pure view (in `src/components/ui/`) and a data-fetching container (in `src/components/`). View components have co-located stories covering all visual states. MSW handlers from the existing `src/mocks/handlers.ts` are reused in Storybook via `msw-storybook-addon`, enabling container-level stories that test the full fetch lifecycle without a running backend.

Development is done in parallel: for each component, the view is extracted and its stories written before moving to the next component. The split and stories ship together, not in two separate passes.

## User Stories

1. As a developer, I want to render `ProductCatalogView` in isolation so that I can work on card layout without running the full app.
2. As a developer, I want a `Loading` story for `ProductCatalogView` so that I can validate the skeleton placeholder layout against the loaded state.
3. As a developer, I want an `Empty` story for `ProductCatalogView` so that I can verify the empty state copy and layout are intentional.
4. As a developer, I want a `Default` story for `ProductCatalogView` with four products so that I can validate the three-column grid at standard density.
5. As a developer, I want a `NoImages` story for `ProductCatalogView` so that I can verify the fallback placeholder renders correctly when `imageUrl` is absent.
6. As a developer, I want to render `PublisherNavView` in isolation so that I can develop navigation hierarchy without a router context.
7. As a developer, I want a `Loading` story for `PublisherNavView` so that I can validate the skeleton state for publisher links.
8. As a developer, I want a `Default` story for `PublisherNavView` so that I can see all publishers and their game children with no active state.
9. As a developer, I want a `PublisherActive` story for `PublisherNavView` so that I can verify the accent dot and active background render correctly for the active publisher.
10. As a developer, I want a `GameActive` story for `PublisherNavView` so that I can verify both the active publisher and active game child are correctly highlighted.
11. As a developer, I want to render `PublisherPageView` in isolation so that I can validate the publisher header layout and accent top bar.
12. As a developer, I want a `Loading` story for `PublisherPageView` so that I can verify skeleton placeholders match the loaded header shape.
13. As a developer, I want a `Loaded` story for `PublisherPageView` with a real accent color so that I can verify the 2px accent bar and publisher avatar render correctly.
14. As a developer, I want to render `GamePageView` in isolation so that I can validate the game header and publisher subtitle.
15. As a developer, I want a `Loading` story for `GamePageView` so that I can verify the skeleton placeholder matches the loaded heading shape.
16. As a developer, I want a `Loaded` story for `GamePageView` so that I can verify the game name headline and publisher subtitle render together correctly.
17. As a developer, I want all stories to render in dark mode by default so that stories match the production app's locked dark theme.
18. As a developer, I want `renderLink` prop injection on all view components so that stories have no TanStack Router dependency and render without a router context.
19. As a developer, I want `msw-storybook-addon` configured globally so that MSW handlers from `src/mocks/handlers.ts` can be imported and reused in container-level stories.
20. As a developer, I want Storybook to use `@storybook/addon-interactions` so that interaction tests can be written as the component library grows.
21. As a designer, I want visual documentation of all component states so that I can review component behaviour without access to a running environment.
22. As a developer, I want the view/container split to follow a consistent file structure so that the pattern is predictable as new components are added.

## Implementation Decisions

- **Component split structure**: View (dumb) components live in `src/components/ui/`. Container (data-fetching) components remain in `src/components/`. Containers import their corresponding view from `ui/`.

- **Router decoupling via `renderLink` prop**: View components that render navigation links (`PublisherNavView`) accept a `renderLink: (href: string, children: ReactNode) => ReactNode` prop instead of importing `Link` from `@tanstack/react-router`. The container passes the real TanStack `Link`. Stories pass a plain `<a>` element. This breaks the router context dependency in all view stories.

- **Co-located stories**: Story files live next to their view component in `src/components/ui/`. `ProductCatalogView.tsx` and `ProductCatalogView.stories.tsx` are in the same directory.

- **MSW reuse**: The existing handlers in `src/mocks/handlers.ts` are imported directly into container-level stories via `msw-storybook-addon`. No new mock data is authored for Storybook ‚Äî the single source of mock truth remains in `handlers.ts`.

- **Storybook configuration**: Storybook v8 with `@storybook/react-vite` framework (matches the Vite build). Global decorator in `.storybook/preview.ts` wraps all stories in `ChakraProvider` with the custom `system` from `src/theme.ts` and sets `data-theme="dark"` to match production.

- **Parallel development workflow**: For each component, the view is extracted and stories are written before moving to the next component. The sequence per component is: extract view ‚Üí update container to use view ‚Üí write stories ‚Üí move to next component.

- **Visual regression**: `@storybook/addon-interactions` is installed for interaction tests. Chromatic is explicitly deferred ‚Äî no visual regression CI is wired at this stage.

- **Theme**: Dark mode only. No `@storybook/addon-themes` toolbar toggle. The app has no light mode; stories should not document a mode that does not exist.

- **View component states to cover**:

| View | Stories |
|---|---|
| `ProductCatalogView` | `Loading`, `Empty`, `Default`, `NoImages` |
| `PublisherNavView` | `Loading`, `Default`, `PublisherActive`, `GameActive` |
| `PublisherPageView` | `Loading`, `Loaded` |
| `GamePageView` | `Loading`, `Loaded` |

## Testing Decisions

A good story tests visual state and prop contracts, not internal implementation. Stories should be written against the view component's public prop interface only ‚Äî no reaching into query hooks, no asserting on internal DOM structure.

- **`ProductCatalogView`**: Stories cover all four visual states by passing different `products` prop values (empty array, array without `imageUrl`, array with full data) and a `isLoading` flag. Prior art: the existing `isLoading` / `isError` branching in `ProductCatalog.tsx` defines the state space.

- **`PublisherNavView`**: Stories cover active state permutations by passing `activePublisherSlug` and `activeGameSlug` props with varying values. Prior art: `PublisherNav.tsx` active state logic.

- **`PublisherPageView` / `GamePageView`**: Stories exercise the accent color prop and loading skeleton. Prior art: the `--accent` CSS variable and skeleton branching in current page components.

- **No interaction tests at this stage**: `@storybook/addon-interactions` is installed but interaction stories (`play` functions) are out of scope for the initial Storybook setup. They are introduced per-component as behaviour grows.

## Out of Scope

- **Chromatic / Percy visual regression CI**: deferred until there is budget or the component library is large enough to justify automated baselines.
- **Light mode stories**: the app is dark-mode locked; no light mode toggle or `prefers-color-scheme` stories.
- **Container-level stories using `RouterProvider`**: view stories cover all visual states. Container stories with a real in-memory router are deferred until navigation behaviour needs regression coverage.
- **`@storybook/addon-a11y`**: accessibility auditing in Storybook is deferred; ARIA compliance is handled at the Chakra UI primitive level.
- **Storybook deployment**: no static Storybook deploy (GitHub Pages, Vercel) in this iteration.
- **New components**: this PRD covers only the four components that exist at time of writing. New components added in S3‚ÄìS8 stories are out of scope here.

## Further Notes

The `renderLink` prop pattern established here becomes the project-wide convention for any view component that renders internal navigation. Document this in a short ADR once the pattern is proven across the four components.

The `msw-storybook-addon` version must be aligned with the MSW v2 API already used in `src/mocks/browser.ts` ‚Äî use `msw-storybook-addon@2.x`, not `1.x`.

title: DS-4: Form control primitives
description:
## Parent

#11 ‚Äî PRD: Design System ‚Äî Chakra UI v3 Component Foundation

## What to build

A reusable `FormField` component that wraps Chakra's `Field`, `Label`, `HelperText`, and `ErrorMessage` into a single composable primitive with accessible validation states. This is the building block for all forms in the storefront ‚Äî Clerk auth forms (S6) and the checkout address form (S7).

`FormField` accepts: `label`, `helperText` (optional), `error` (optional string ‚Äî renders `ErrorMessage` and marks the field invalid when present), and `children` (the input element).

Deliver a demo form with at least one text input and one select that shows valid, helper-text, and error states ‚Äî so the component is visually and behaviourally verifiable.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `FormField` renders `Label` above its `children` input
- [ ] When `helperText` is provided, it is rendered and associated with the input via `aria-describedby`
- [ ] When `error` is provided, `ErrorMessage` is rendered, the input receives `aria-invalid="true"`, and `helperText` is hidden
- [ ] When `error` is absent, the field is in a valid state with no error markup in the DOM
- [ ] `FormField` is testable via `renderWithProviders` ‚Äî assert on label text, error message text, and `aria-invalid` attribute
- [ ] All existing tests continue to pass

## Blocked by

#12 ‚Äî DS-1: Theme extension config

title: DS-3: Drawer component pattern
description:
## Parent

#11 ‚Äî PRD: Design System ‚Äî Chakra UI v3 Component Foundation

## What to build

A reusable `AppDrawer` wrapper around Chakra's `Drawer` with correct focus trap configuration and dark-theme styling. The Cart in S5 will use this as its side panel. Establishing the pattern now prevents ad-hoc implementations diverging across stories.

`AppDrawer` accepts: `isOpen`, `onClose`, `title`, and `children`. Focus is trapped inside while open and returned to the trigger element on close. The overlay dismisses the drawer on click.

This slice delivers a demo route or Storybook-style page that opens and closes the drawer so it is visually verifiable.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `AppDrawer` component renders a Chakra `Drawer` from the right side with a header title, close button, and scrollable body
- [ ] Focus is trapped inside `AppDrawer` while open
- [ ] Focus returns to the trigger element when `AppDrawer` closes
- [ ] Clicking the overlay closes the drawer
- [ ] `AppDrawer` is testable via `renderWithProviders` ‚Äî open state renders children, closed state does not
- [ ] All existing tests continue to pass

## Blocked by

#12 ‚Äî DS-1: Theme extension config

title: DS-2: Toaster / notification system
description:
## Parent

#11 ‚Äî PRD: Design System ‚Äî Chakra UI v3 Component Foundation

## What to build

Configure Chakra's `Toaster` at the app root and establish the `toaster.create()` call pattern. The Toaster must be mounted once (in `__root.tsx`) and accessible from any component via a shared `toaster` instance. Notifications must be announced to screen readers via ARIA live regions (Chakra handles this automatically when configured correctly).

This is a pure infrastructure slice ‚Äî no feature uses it yet. Verify it works by rendering a test notification in the Product catalog page temporarily (then remove), or via a dedicated test.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `Toaster` is mounted once in `__root.tsx`
- [ ] A shared `toaster` instance is exported from a single module for use across the codebase
- [ ] A toast can be created with `toaster.create({ title, type })` from any component
- [ ] Toasts are announced to screen readers (ARIA `role="status"` or `role="alert"` on the container)
- [ ] All existing tests continue to pass

## Blocked by

#12 ‚Äî DS-1: Theme extension config

title: DS-1: Theme extension config ‚Äî createSystem with brand token placeholders
description:
## Parent

#11 ‚Äî PRD: Design System ‚Äî Chakra UI v3 Component Foundation

## What to build

Replace `defaultSystem` with a custom `createSystem` call that extends the default Chakra config with placeholder brand tokens (accent color, font family, border radii). No visual change to the app ‚Äî the goal is establishing the extension point so future brand tokens can be swapped in without touching individual components.

The `ChakraProvider` in `__root.tsx` and `renderWithProviders` in `test-utils.tsx` both receive the new system object instead of `defaultSystem`.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] A custom system is exported from a single config module (e.g. `src/theme.ts`) and used in both `ChakraProvider` and `renderWithProviders`
- [ ] Placeholder tokens are defined for: primary accent color, heading font family, body font family, and default border radius
- [ ] All 5 existing tests pass with the new system
- [ ] No visual change to the Product catalog page

## Blocked by

None ‚Äî can start immediately.

title: PRD: Design System ‚Äî Chakra UI v3 Component Foundation
description:
## Problem Statement

Buyers interacting with the storefront across catalog browsing, cart management, checkout, and authentication expect accessible, consistent UI ‚Äî keyboard navigation, ARIA labels, focus management, and screen reader support. Building these guarantees from scratch using Tailwind utility classes is error-prone and time-intensive. Tailwind ships no component primitives: every Modal, Select, Toast, and Drawer must be hand-rolled, with accessibility as the developer's sole responsibility. As the storefront grows from S2 through S8, the compounding cost of building inaccessible primitives and retrofitting them becomes a significant liability.

## Solution

Chakra UI v3 replaces Tailwind CSS as the design system. Chakra ships fully accessible, composable components built on Ark UI, with a token-based theming system. All new components are built using Chakra primitives. The app defaults to a dark theme matching the esports aesthetic. The default Chakra system token set is used now; brand tokens will be layered on top via `createSystem` when a design identity is established.

## User Stories

1. As a Buyer, I want interactive elements (modals, selects, drawers) to be keyboard-navigable, so that I can use the storefront without a mouse.
2. As a Buyer using a screen reader, I want all UI components to have correct ARIA roles and labels, so that I can navigate and use the storefront independently.
3. As a Buyer, I want focus to be managed correctly when modals and drawers open and close, so that I am never lost in the page.
4. As a Buyer, I want a consistent dark-themed UI across all pages, so that the storefront feels cohesive and on-brand for esports.
5. As a Buyer on mobile, I want touch-friendly tap targets and responsive layouts, so that the storefront is usable on a phone.
6. As a Buyer, I want loading states (skeletons) on product cards while data fetches, so that the layout does not shift or flash.
7. As a Buyer, I want error states to be clearly communicated with accessible color and text, so that I understand what went wrong.
8. As a Buyer, I want hover and focus states on interactive elements to be clearly visible, so that I always know what I am about to activate.
9. As a Buyer, I want form inputs (address, card number, quantity) to have visible labels and validation messages, so that I can complete checkout without confusion.
10. As a Buyer, I want toast notifications for cart actions (added, removed, updated) to be announced to screen readers, so that I know the action succeeded without a visual check.
11. As a Buyer, I want the Cart drawer to trap focus while open, so that I cannot accidentally interact with content behind it.
12. As a Buyer, I want the color contrast of all text to meet WCAG AA standards, so that I can read the storefront in varied lighting conditions.

## Implementation Decisions

- **Design system**: Chakra UI v3 (`@chakra-ui/react` 3.x). Tailwind CSS removed entirely ‚Äî no coexistence, no utility class fallback.
- **Theme**: Default Chakra system (`defaultSystem`) used now. Custom brand tokens extend `createSystem` when design identity is established ‚Äî no component rewrites required, only token overrides.
- **Color mode**: Dark default via `data-theme="dark"` on `<html>`. No user toggle for now.
- **Provider placement**: `ChakraProvider value={defaultSystem}` wraps children inside `RootDocument` in `__root.tsx`.
- **Component rule**: All new components use Chakra primitives (`Box`, `SimpleGrid`, `LinkBox`, `Skeleton`, `Card`, `Modal`, `Drawer`, etc.). Raw HTML elements with ad-hoc styling are a code-smell in this codebase.
- **Accessible card links**: Product cards use `LinkBox` + `LinkOverlay` for a single, accessible hit area per card.
- **Loading states**: `Skeleton` from Chakra replaces custom pulse animation divs.
- **ADR**: Decision recorded in `docs/adr/0007-chakra-ui-design-system.md`.

## Testing Decisions

- **What makes a good test**: Assert on externally visible behavior ‚Äî rendered text, accessible roles, and user interactions. Never assert on Chakra prop values, class names, or component internals.
- **Seam**: `renderWithProviders` in `src/test-utils.tsx`. Wraps the component under test in `ChakraProvider` + `QueryClientProvider`. This is the single test seam for all component tests; no lower seam is needed.
- **Prior art**: `src/routes/index.test.tsx` ‚Äî renders `ProductCatalog`, waits for MSW-intercepted data, asserts on product names in the DOM. All future component tests follow this pattern.
- **Accessibility assertions**: Use `@testing-library` queries by role (`getByRole('article')`, `getByRole('link', { name })`) rather than by class or test ID.

## Out of Scope

- Custom brand color palette or typography tokens (deferred until design identity is defined).
- Light mode or user-controlled color mode toggle.
- Visual regression testing (e.g. Chromatic, Percy).
- Chakra theming for third-party components (Clerk auth UI, Stripe Elements ‚Äî these have their own theming APIs).

## Further Notes

The migration from Tailwind to Chakra was performed as a big-bang switch at S1 (one component existed at migration time), keeping the codebase free of coexistence debt. All stories from S2 onward build on Chakra exclusively. When brand tokens are introduced, they extend `createSystem` in a single config file without touching individual components.

title: S8: Account order history
description:
## Parent

#1

## What to build

An authenticated-only route (`/account/orders`) listing the signed-in Buyer's past Orders. Each Order shows the Order ID, date, SKUs purchased, and total amount. Unauthenticated Buyers who navigate to this route are redirected to `/sign-in`.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `/account/orders` is only accessible to authenticated Buyers; guests are redirected to `/sign-in`
- [ ] Order list renders Order ID, date, line items (SKU name + quantity), and Order total
- [ ] An empty state is shown when the Account has no Orders
- [ ] Route-level test: MSW seeds two Orders for an authenticated Buyer; test asserts both Orders are listed with correct details

## Blocked by

- #9

title: S7: Checkout + Stripe payment + Order confirmation
description:
## Parent

#1

## What to build

A checkout route (`/checkout`) where the Buyer enters a shipping address and pays. Stripe Elements handles the payment UI. The frontend calls the backend to create an Order and receive a Stripe PaymentIntent client secret, then confirms the payment client-side. On success, the Buyer is shown an Order confirmation page with an Order ID.

Guest Buyers complete checkout without an Account. The checkout form collects their email for the Order record.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `/checkout` is reachable from the Cart view
- [ ] Shipping address form validates required fields before allowing payment
- [ ] Stripe Elements card field is rendered and accepts test card numbers
- [ ] Submitting with a valid test card transitions to the Order confirmation page
- [ ] Confirmation page shows Order ID and a summary of purchased SKUs
- [ ] Submitting with a declined test card shows an inline payment error without losing form state
- [ ] Route-level test: MSW mocks the Order creation endpoint; Stripe is mocked; test asserts confirmation page renders after successful submission

## Blocked by

- #8

title: S5: Cart (add, update quantity, remove, subtotal)
description:
## Parent

#1

## What to build

A Cart that accumulates SKUs across the session. The "Add to Cart" button on the product detail page adds the selected SKU. A cart view (sidebar or `/cart` route) shows all Cart items with quantity controls, remove buttons, and a running subtotal.

Guest Buyers carry their Cart in `sessionStorage`. Authenticated Buyers have their Cart synced to the backend on login (merge guest Cart into account Cart). Cart item count is shown in the nav.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Adding a SKU from the product detail page increments the Cart item count in the nav
- [ ] Cart view lists each SKU with name, variant, price, and quantity
- [ ] Incrementing or decrementing quantity updates the subtotal immediately
- [ ] Removing an item removes it from the Cart and updates the subtotal
- [ ] Guest Cart survives page refresh (persisted in `sessionStorage`)
- [ ] Signing in merges any guest Cart items into the Account Cart
- [ ] Route-level test: adds two SKUs, updates a quantity, removes one, asserts correct subtotal

## Blocked by

- #7

title: S6: Clerk auth (sign-up, sign-in, guest‚Üíaccount upgrade)
description:
## Parent

#1

## What to build

Buyer authentication via Clerk. Adds sign-up and sign-in routes using Clerk's prebuilt React components. A Buyer can create an Account or sign in at any point. The app distinguishes between Guest and Account holder states throughout the UI (nav shows sign-in link for guests, account menu for authenticated Buyers).

Clerk session tokens are attached as Bearer tokens on all API requests so the backend can verify identity when it ships.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `/sign-in` and `/sign-up` routes render Clerk UI components
- [ ] Signing in transitions the nav from guest state to account state without full page reload
- [ ] Signing out returns the Buyer to guest state
- [ ] All API requests made while authenticated include the Clerk Bearer token in the `Authorization` header
- [ ] Route-level test: mocks Clerk session; asserts authenticated nav items are visible when signed in and hidden when signed out

## Blocked by

- #6

title: S4: Product detail page + SKU selection
description:
## Parent

#1

## What to build

A product detail route (`/[publisher]/[game]/products/[slug]`) showing full product information and a SKU picker. The Buyer selects variant dimensions (size, colour, edition) to identify the specific SKU they want. Unavailable SKUs are visually distinct and cannot be selected. The selected SKU's price updates to match the chosen variant.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Route renders product name, images, description, and all variant dimensions
- [ ] Selecting variant options highlights the matching SKU
- [ ] Unavailable SKUs are greyed out and non-interactive
- [ ] Price shown reflects the selected SKU's price
- [ ] An "Add to Cart" button is present and enabled only when a valid, available SKU is selected
- [ ] Route-level test: MSW seeds a Product with mixed available/unavailable SKUs; test asserts unavailable variants cannot be selected and price updates on selection

## Blocked by

- #3

title: S3: Facet filtering (Game / Team / Character)
description:
## Parent

#1

## What to build

A filter panel on the catalog that lets Buyers narrow Products by the three orthogonal facets: Game, Team, and Character. Filter selections are stored in URL query params (e.g. `?game=lol&team=t1&character=jinx`) so filtered results are shareable and survive page refresh.

The MSW handler reads the query params and returns only matching Products. Multiple facets can be active simultaneously.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Filter panel renders checkboxes (or toggles) for Game, Team, and Character
- [ ] Selecting a facet updates the URL and narrows the Product grid without full page reload
- [ ] Multiple facets can be combined (AND logic across facets)
- [ ] Clearing all filters restores the full catalog
- [ ] Sharing the URL with filter params renders the same filtered view
- [ ] Route-level test: simulates clicking a Team filter, asserts only matching Products remain visible

## Blocked by

- #3

title: S2: Publisher & Game navigation
description:
## Parent

#1

## What to build

Publisher and Game navigation so Buyers can scope the catalog to a specific brand. Adds two routes:

- `/[publisher]` ‚Äî shows Products for one Publisher, with Publisher branding (logo, brand accent colour)
- `/[publisher]/[game]` ‚Äî shows Products for one Game within that Publisher

A navigation sidebar or top nav lists available Publishers and their Games. Active Publisher/Game is visually highlighted. Brand accent colours are applied via Tailwind theme variables keyed to the publisher slug.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `/riot` renders only Riot Games Products
- [ ] `/riot/league-of-legends` renders only LoL Products
- [ ] Publisher logo and brand accent colour are visible on the page
- [ ] Nav lists all Publishers; expanding a Publisher shows its Games
- [ ] Navigating between Publishers/Games does not trigger a full page reload (SPA navigation)
- [ ] Route-level test: MSW seeds multiple publishers; test asserts correct Products shown per publisher route

## Blocked by

- #3

title: S1: Product catalog page
description:
## Parent

#1

## What to build

A catalog route (`/`) that fetches and displays a grid of Products from the API. Each Product card shows the product name, image, starting price, and a link to the product detail page. The page uses the dark gaming aesthetic established in the Tailwind theme.

TanStack Query manages the fetch lifecycle. MSW returns a list of fake Products. Loading and error states are handled and visible to the Buyer.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Navigating to `/` renders a grid of Product cards
- [ ] Each card shows: product name, image placeholder, price, and a link to the detail page
- [ ] A loading skeleton is shown while data fetches
- [ ] An error message is shown when the API returns an error (MSW can simulate this)
- [ ] Route-level test: renders the catalog with MSW-seeded Products and asserts card content is visible

## Blocked by

- #2

title: F1: Foundation ‚Äî MSW + typed API client + test scaffold
description:
## Parent

#1

## What to build

Set up the shared infrastructure every other slice depends on:

1. **MSW** ‚Äî install and configure Mock Service Worker. Service worker registered in `src/main.tsx` (dev only). A `src/mocks/` directory holds handler definitions, one file per resource (products, publishers, games, orders). Handlers mirror the future OpenAPI spec shape.
2. **Typed API client** ‚Äî install `openapi-typescript` and `openapi-fetch`. Add a placeholder `openapi.yaml` spec at repo root with at least one endpoint stub so the generated types are non-empty. Wire up a typed `client` singleton at `src/api/client.ts`.
3. **Test render utility** ‚Äî a helper at `src/test-utils.tsx` that wraps components with TanStack Router and TanStack Query providers, pre-seeded with MSW. All route-level tests import from here instead of `@testing-library/react` directly.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `pnpm dev` starts with MSW active and logs "MSW: enabled" in the browser console
- [ ] `pnpm test` passes with at least one smoke test that renders the root route using the test utility
- [ ] `src/api/client.ts` exports a typed client generated from `openapi.yaml`
- [ ] Adding a new MSW handler in `src/mocks/` is the only change needed to mock a new endpoint in both dev and tests

## Blocked by

None ‚Äî can start immediately.

title: PRD: Esport Merch Shop ‚Äî Initial Storefront
description:
## Problem Statement

Esport fans want to buy officially licensed merchandise for their favourite games, teams, and characters ‚Äî but no single storefront spans multiple publishers and lets them filter by game, team, and character simultaneously. Buyers are forced to visit scattered publisher stores, each with different UX, checkout flows, and product ranges.

## Solution

A dark-themed, multi-publisher licensed merch storefront where Buyers browse Products by Game, Team, and Character facets, add SKUs to a Cart, and check out as a Guest or authenticated Account holder. Orders are fulfilled via drop-ship; no warehouse inventory is managed.

## User Stories

1. As a Buyer, I want to browse the full product catalog, so that I can discover available merch.
2. As a Buyer, I want to filter Products by Game, so that I only see merch for titles I care about.
3. As a Buyer, I want to filter Products by Team, so that I can find merch for my favourite esport organisation.
4. As a Buyer, I want to filter Products by Character, so that I can find merch featuring my favourite in-game figure.
5. As a Buyer, I want to combine Game, Team, and Character filters simultaneously, so that I can narrow results precisely (e.g. LoL + T1 + a specific character).
6. As a Buyer, I want to view a Product page with all available SKUs (size, colour, edition), so that I can choose the exact variant I want.
7. As a Buyer, I want to see the price and Availability of each SKU before adding it to my Cart, so that I do not attempt to purchase unavailable items.
8. As a Buyer, I want to add a SKU to my Cart, so that I can accumulate multiple items before paying.
9. As a Buyer, I want to view my Cart with a summary of SKUs, quantities, and subtotal, so that I can review my intended purchase.
10. As a Buyer, I want to update the quantity of a Cart item, so that I can buy more than one of the same SKU.
11. As a Buyer, I want to remove an item from my Cart, so that I can change my mind without abandoning the entire Cart.
12. As a Buyer, I want to proceed to checkout without creating an Account, so that I can complete my purchase quickly as a Guest.
13. As a Buyer, I want to optionally create an Account during or after checkout, so that I can access order history later.
14. As an Account holder, I want to sign in to my Account, so that my Cart persists across sessions.
15. As an Account holder, I want to view my Order history, so that I can track past purchases.
16. As a Buyer, I want to enter my shipping address at checkout, so that my Order is delivered to the right location.
17. As a Buyer, I want to pay via card, Apple Pay, or Google Pay using Stripe, so that I can use my preferred payment method.
18. As a Buyer, I want to receive an Order confirmation after payment, so that I know my purchase was successful.
19. As a Buyer, I want to browse Products by Publisher (e.g. Riot Games, Valve), so that I can explore merch within a specific brand.
20. As a Buyer, I want to see Publisher branding and Game artwork on product pages, so that the licensed nature of the merch is clear.
21. As a Buyer, I want to navigate the shop on mobile, so that I can browse and buy on my phone.
22. As a Buyer, I want fast page loads with SSR, so that the catalog is immediately visible without a loading spinner.
23. As a Buyer, I want to see a loading state when data is fetching, so that the UI feels responsive.
24. As a Buyer, I want to see a clear error state when the API is unavailable, so that I am not left looking at a blank page.
25. As a Buyer, I want SKUs that are unavailable to be visually indicated and non-purchasable, so that I do not select a variant I cannot buy.

## Implementation Decisions

- **Stack**: TanStack Start (React 19, TanStack Router, TanStack Query), Tailwind CSS v4, Clerk for auth, Stripe for payment. REST + OpenAPI contract to a separately hosted backend.
- **API layer**: All data fetching via `fetch` against an OpenAPI-specified REST API. No server functions that touch a database. A generated typed client (e.g. `openapi-typescript` + `openapi-fetch`) wraps all calls.
- **MSW for development**: MSW intercepts all API calls in the browser during development and in tests. Handlers mirror the OpenAPI spec. Removing MSW when the real backend ships requires no changes to component or hook code.
- **Domain hierarchy**: `Publisher ‚Üí Game ‚Üí Product`. URL structure reflects this: `/[publisher]/[game]/products/[slug]`.
- **Facets**: Products carry three orthogonal facets ‚Äî Game, Team, Character. Filter state lives in the URL as query params so results are shareable.
- **SKU model**: Each purchasable variant (size + colour + edition combination) is a distinct SKU with its own price and Availability flag (boolean, not a stock count).
- **Cart**: Cart state is managed client-side (TanStack Store or React context). Authenticated Buyers sync their Cart to the backend on login. Guests carry Cart in `sessionStorage`.
- **Auth**: Clerk handles sign-up, sign-in, and session. Guest-to-Account upgrade is supported. Clerk JWTs are forwarded as Bearer tokens to the backend.
- **Checkout**: Stripe Elements for payment UI. On Order submission, the frontend calls the backend to create an Order and obtain a Stripe PaymentIntent client secret, then confirms the payment client-side.
- **UI theme**: Dark gaming aesthetic. Publisher and Game brand colours used as accent tokens. Tailwind CSS v4 custom theme variables per publisher.
- **Testing seam**: Route-level integration tests only. Render full TanStack Router route components with React Testing Library + MSW. MSW handlers are shared between dev mode and tests.

## Testing Decisions

A good test renders a full route, simulates Buyer interactions (clicks, form fills), and asserts on visible output (text, disabled states, navigation). It does not assert on component internals, query keys, or store shape.

Modules to test at the route level:
- Product catalog route (filter, list, pagination)
- Product detail route (SKU selection, add to Cart)
- Cart route (quantity update, remove, subtotal)
- Checkout route (address entry, payment, confirmation)
- Account sign-in / sign-up flows (Clerk UI integration)
- Order history route (authenticated only)

No prior art in the codebase ‚Äî this is a greenfield repo. MSW setup and a test utility that wraps TanStack Router + Query providers will be the shared scaffolding for all route-level tests.

## Out of Scope

- Backend implementation (separate project)
- Inventory management, stock counts, or warehouse operations
- Admin / publisher portal for managing Products and SKUs
- Pre-orders, backorders, or waitlists
- Discount codes, promotions, or loyalty programmes
- Product reviews or ratings
- Internationalisation (i18n) and localisation beyond English
- Native mobile app

## Further Notes

- ADRs 0001‚Äì0006 in `docs/adr/` record the key architectural decisions made during design.
- `CONTEXT.md` at the repo root defines the canonical vocabulary ‚Äî use it when naming routes, components, types, and API endpoints.
- `Character` is the canonical term for in-game figures across all publishers; never use Champion, Hero, or Agent in code or copy.
